meta.title: Java/Kotlin + Protobuf Monorepo
meta.description: Review checklist for Java, Kotlin, and Protobuf monorepo projects

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change, the problem it solves, and any relevant context a reviewer needs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code. A clear description speeds up review, reduces misunderstandings, and creates a searchable historical record.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code? Is there a link to a ticket or design doc?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add more context to the PR description? It would help to know why we are changing the serialization logic - is this fixing a specific incident?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves more reviewer time than it costs to write.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately, or should it be split into smaller PRs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a bug fix with a refactor with a new feature makes review harder and rollbacks riskier.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large, multi-concern PRs are slow to review, prone to rubber-stamping, and difficult to revert if one part causes issues. Smaller PRs get faster, higher-quality reviews.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently? Does the diff include both feature work and unrelated cleanup?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new gRPC endpoint and also refactors the DAO layer. Could we split these into two PRs? The refactor could land first and the endpoint can build on it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need a paragraph to explain what it does, it is probably too big.

items.text: Are there any unrelated changes bundled in (drive-by fixes, formatting-only diffs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or unrelated tweaks included alongside the main change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual diff, make git blame less useful, and complicate reverts. They also make the reviewer's job harder by mixing signal with noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Scan the file list. Are there files that do not seem related to the PR's stated purpose? Are there formatting-only changes in files otherwise untouched?
baseContent.exampleComment: The import reordering in UserRepository.java looks unrelated to the caching changes. Mind pulling those into a separate PR so this one stays focused?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs focused. Save drive-by fixes for a dedicated cleanup PR.

items.text: Does the commit history tell a coherent story? Are commits atomic and well-messaged?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commits should be logically organized with clear messages. Each commit should represent a meaningful, atomic unit of work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit history helps future developers understand why changes were made, simplifies bisecting bugs, and makes reverts surgical instead of all-or-nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through the commit log. Are messages descriptive? Are there WIP or fixup commits that should have been squashed? Does the sequence make logical sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit history has several 'WIP' and 'fix' commits. Could you squash these into logical units before merge? It will make the history much easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commits for the person who will read git log in 6 months.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code that is no longer used should be deleted, not commented out. Version control preserves history if you ever need it back.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion - is it temporarily disabled? Is it a reference? It adds noise and often never gets cleaned up.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for large blocks of commented-out code. Check for unused methods, imports, or fields flagged by IntelliJ or the compiler.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out block has been here since the original implementation. Since we have git history, let us remove it to keep things clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers so you do not have to.

items.text: Are TODO/FIXME comments accompanied by a tracking ticket?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TODO and FIXME comments should reference a ticket number so the work is actually tracked and not forgotten.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untracked TODOs accumulate as tech debt that nobody owns. With a ticket, the work enters the backlog and can be prioritized.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for TODO, FIXME, HACK, or XXX comments. Do they include a ticket reference (e.g., JIRA-1234)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This TODO does not have a tracking ticket. Could you file one and reference it here? Otherwise it will likely get forgotten.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// TODO: handle pagination
List<User> results = userDao.fetchAll();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// TODO(PROJ-1234): add pagination support for large result sets
List<User> results = userDao.fetchAll();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every TODO should have an owner. A ticket number is that owner.

items.text: Are class, method, and variable names descriptive and consistent with project conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Names should clearly convey purpose. Class names should be nouns, method names should be verbs, and variable names should describe what they hold.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code is read far more often than it is written. Descriptive names reduce cognitive load and make code self-documenting, reducing the need for comments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through the new names introduced. Could a teammate understand their purpose without additional context? Do they follow Java naming conventions?
baseContent.exampleComment: The variable name 'dt' is ambiguous - could you rename it to 'deliveryTimestamp' or 'departureTime' to clarify its purpose?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Good names make code readable without comments. Invest time in naming.

items.text: Do method names clearly describe their behavior (e.g., `findUserById` vs `getUser`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A method name should tell you what it does without reading the body. It should describe the action and, where useful, hint at side effects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misleading method names are a source of bugs. If a method named 'getUser' also modifies state, callers will be surprised. Clear names prevent misuse.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read each new method name. Does it accurately describe what the method does? Are side effects apparent from the name?
baseContent.exampleComment: The method 'processData' is too generic. Since it validates and persists the order, could we rename it to 'validateAndSaveOrder'?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Method names are a contract. Make sure the implementation matches.

items.text: Are boolean variables/methods named with `is`, `has`, `should`, `can` prefixes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Boolean variables and methods should use prefixes like is, has, can, should, or was to make their true/false nature obvious.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A boolean named 'active' is ambiguous - is it a status enum or a flag? Naming it 'isActive' immediately conveys that it holds a true/false value and reads naturally in conditionals.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check boolean fields and methods in the diff. Do they use conventional prefixes? Do they read naturally in if-statements?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field 'enabled' would be clearer as 'isEnabled' - it reads more naturally in conditionals like 'if (config.isEnabled())'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Boolean names should read naturally as true/false questions.

items.text: Are constants in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` and class names in `PascalCase`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Java conventions: classes use PascalCase, methods and variables use camelCase, constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE, and packages use lowercase.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent casing conventions let developers instantly distinguish constants from variables, classes from methods, and packages from types. Deviating causes confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new identifiers in the diff. Are constants declared as static final in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE? Are class names PascalCase? Are method names camelCase?
baseContent.exampleComment: The constant 'maxRetries' should be 'MAX_RETRIES' since it is declared as static final.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow Java naming conventions consistently - they exist for instant recognition.

items.text: Are abbreviations avoided in names unless they are universally understood?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Names should use full words rather than abbreviations, unless the abbreviation is universally understood (e.g., URL, HTTP, ID).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Abbreviations force readers to decode meaning from context. What does 'mgr' mean - manager, merger? Full words are unambiguous and IDE autocomplete makes them just as fast to type.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for abbreviated names in the diff. Are they universally understood? Would a new team member know what they mean?
baseContent.exampleComment: The variable 'usrMgr' would be clearer as 'userManager'. Abbreviations save keystrokes but cost readability.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Spell it out. Your IDE handles the typing; humans handle the reading.

items.text: Is the class doing too much? Does it violate the Single Responsibility Principle?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each class should have a single, well-defined responsibility. If a class handles authentication, data transformation, and email sending, it is doing too much.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Classes with multiple responsibilities are hard to test, hard to reuse, and change for multiple unrelated reasons. The Single Responsibility Principle keeps code maintainable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Can you describe the class in one sentence without using 'and'? Does it have more than a handful of dependencies? Are unrelated methods grouped together?
baseContent.exampleComment: This OrderService class handles validation, persistence, notification, and metrics. Could we extract notification logic into an OrderNotificationService?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One class, one reason to change. Delegate distinct concerns to collaborators.

items.text: Are fields and methods using the narrowest possible access modifier (`private` > `package-private` > `protected` > `public`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Fields, methods, and classes should use the most restrictive access modifier possible: private over package-private, package-private over protected, protected over public.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broad access creates implicit APIs that others depend on, making refactoring dangerous. Narrow access limits the surface area of change and communicates intent clearly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new public and protected members. Do they need to be public? Could they be package-private or private? Are internal helper methods accidentally exposed?
baseContent.exampleComment: The method 'buildCacheKey' is declared public but only called within this class. Could we make it private to limit the API surface?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start private. Widen access only when you have a concrete reason.

items.text: Is inheritance used appropriately, or would composition be a better fit?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Inheritance creates a tight coupling between parent and child. Composition (having a reference to another object) is usually more flexible and testable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile - changes to a base class ripple through all subclasses. Composition lets you swap implementations and test components in isolation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for new subclasses. Does the child truly represent an 'is-a' relationship? Would a strategy pattern, delegation, or interface work better?
baseContent.exampleComment: Rather than extending BaseProcessor, could OrderProcessor compose a ProcessingStrategy? This would let us test and swap strategies independently.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Favor composition over inheritance. Use inheritance only for true 'is-a' relationships.

items.text: Are abstract classes and interfaces used to define contracts, not just to share code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interfaces define contracts that multiple implementations can fulfill. Abstract classes provide shared behavior. Both should be used to define clear extension points.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Programming to interfaces allows swapping implementations without changing callers, enables mocking in tests, and makes the system more extensible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are new classes implementing interfaces or directly coupling to concrete types? Could an interface be introduced to enable future flexibility or testability?
baseContent.exampleComment: The PaymentService is a concrete class injected everywhere. Extracting a PaymentService interface would make it easier to mock in tests and add new payment providers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define contracts with interfaces. Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

items.text: Are utility/helper classes kept to a minimum and well-justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Utility classes (static method collections) should be used sparingly. Often the methods belong on the objects they operate on.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Utility classes are a code smell that often indicates missing domain modeling. They are hard to mock, violate OOP principles, and tend to grow into dumping grounds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new *Utils, *Helper, or *Util classes. Could the methods live on the types they operate on? Is the utility genuinely cross-cutting (like StringUtils)?
baseContent.exampleComment: OrderUtils.calculateTotal(order) would be more natural as order.calculateTotal(). Could we move this method onto the Order class?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put behavior where the data is. Utility classes should be the exception, not the rule.

items.text: Are `Optional` return types used instead of returning `null` from methods?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Methods that might not return a value should return Optional<T> instead of null. This makes the absence of a value explicit in the type system.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning null forces every caller to remember a null check or risk NullPointerException. Optional makes the possibility of absence explicit and provides safe access methods.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for methods that return null to indicate absence. Could they return Optional instead? Check that Optional is not used for fields or parameters (it is meant for return types).
baseContent.exampleComment: This method returns null when the user is not found. Returning Optional<User> would make the caller aware they need to handle the absent case.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Optional for return types to make absence explicit. Never return null from a method that could return Optional.

items.text: Is `Optional.get()` avoided in favor of `orElse()`, `orElseThrow()`, `ifPresent()`, `map()`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Calling Optional.get() without checking isPresent() defeats the purpose of Optional. Use orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow(), or map()/flatMap() instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Optional.get() throws NoSuchElementException if the value is absent - the same class of bug as NullPointerException. The safe methods express intent and handle absence gracefully.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .get() calls on Optional instances. Are they guarded by isPresent()? Could they be replaced with orElse, orElseThrow, or map?
baseContent.exampleComment: This Optional.get() call will throw if the user is absent. Consider using orElseThrow() with a descriptive exception, or map() to transform the value safely.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
Optional<User> user = findUser(id);
String name = user.get().getName(); // throws if absent

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never call Optional.get() without a safety net. Use orElse, orElseThrow, or map instead.

items.text: Are streams used appropriately (not overly complex, not replacing simple loops unnecessarily)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Streams are powerful for data transformation pipelines but can become unreadable when overused. Complex logic should stay in traditional loops.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deeply nested stream chains are harder to debug (no breakpoints mid-stream), harder to read, and can have unexpected performance characteristics. Balance expressiveness with clarity.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look at stream pipelines with more than 3-4 operations. Would a for loop be more readable? Are streams used in performance-critical hot paths where forEach overhead matters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This 8-operation stream chain is hard to follow. Could we extract the filter/map logic into well-named helper methods, or use a for loop for the complex transformation?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
return orders.stream()
    .filter(o -> o.getStatus() != null)
    .filter(o -> o.getStatus().equals("ACTIVE"))
    .map(o -> new AbstractMap.SimpleEntry<>(o.getId(),
        o.getItems().stream()
            .filter(i -> i.getPrice() != null)
            .mapToDouble(i -> i.getPrice().doubleValue())
            .sum()))
    .filter(e -> e.getValue() > 100)
    .collect(Collectors.toMap(Map.Entry::getKey, Map.Entry::getValue));

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Streams are great for simple pipelines. For complex logic, a well-structured loop is often clearer.

items.text: Are `var` declarations used only where the type is obvious from the right-hand side?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Java's var keyword (local variable type inference) should only be used when the type is obvious from the right-hand side of the assignment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: var improves readability when the type is redundant (e.g., var list = new ArrayList<String>()). But var result = process(data) hides the type and forces the reader to look up the method signature.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check each var usage. Is the type obvious from context? Would a reader need to look up a method signature to understand the type?
baseContent.exampleComment: The type of 'var result = service.process(data)' is not obvious. Could we use the explicit type here to improve readability?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
var users = new ArrayList<User>(); // type is obvious
var entry = Map.entry("key", "value"); // type is obvious
OrderResult result = service.processOrder(data); // type is not obvious, be explicit

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use var when the type is obvious. Use explicit types when clarity is needed.

items.text: Is `equals()` and `hashCode()` overridden together when either is overridden?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If you override equals(), you must override hashCode(), and vice versa. Objects that are equal must have the same hash code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Violating this contract breaks HashMap, HashSet, and any hash-based collection. Two equal objects with different hash codes will end up in different buckets, causing subtle and hard-to-debug data loss.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for overridden equals() methods. Is hashCode() also overridden? Are they consistent - using the same fields? Consider using @EqualsAndHashCode from Lombok or IDE generation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This class overrides equals() but not hashCode(). This will cause incorrect behavior if instances are used as HashMap keys or in HashSets.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always override equals() and hashCode() together using the same fields.

items.text: Are immutable objects preferred? Are fields `final` where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objects should be immutable where possible - fields should be final, no setters, and state set only via the constructor.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe, easier to reason about, and eliminate an entire category of bugs related to unexpected mutation. They are also safe to share and cache.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new classes. Are fields declared final? Are there setters that could be removed? Could the class be made immutable with a builder for construction?
baseContent.exampleComment: The fields in this value object are not final. Since the object is not meant to change after creation, could we make the fields final and remove the setters?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make objects immutable by default. Mutability should be a deliberate choice, not the default.

items.text: Are builder patterns used for objects with many constructor parameters?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Classes with many constructor parameters should use the Builder pattern instead of telescoping constructors or setters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Constructors with 4+ parameters are error-prone (easy to swap arguments of the same type) and hard to read. Builders make construction readable and can enforce required vs optional fields.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for constructors with more than 3-4 parameters. Would a builder improve readability? Are there multiple constructor overloads that could be consolidated?
baseContent.exampleComment: This constructor has 7 parameters, several of which are Strings. A builder would make construction less error-prone and more readable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
DatabaseConfig.builder()
    .host("localhost")
    .port(5432)
    .database("mydb")
    .username("admin")
    .password("password")
    .useSsl(true)
    .timeoutSeconds(30)
    .maxConnections(10)
    .timezone("UTC")
    .build();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use builders for objects with many parameters. Named parameters beat positional ones.

items.text: Is `StringBuilder` used for string concatenation in loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: String concatenation with + in loops creates a new String object on each iteration. Use StringBuilder for building strings in loops.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each string concatenation allocates a new String object and copies all previous characters. In a loop of N iterations with strings of length L, this is O(N*L) allocations. StringBuilder avoids this.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation (+=) inside loops. Is StringBuilder or String.join() used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This string concatenation inside the for loop creates a new String object on each iteration. Using StringBuilder would be much more efficient.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use StringBuilder for string concatenation in loops. For simple joining, use String.join().

items.text: Are the correct collection types chosen (`List` vs `Set` vs `Map`, `ArrayList` vs `LinkedList`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Choose the right collection for the job: List for ordered sequences, Set for unique elements, Map for key-value pairs. Choose the right implementation: ArrayList vs LinkedList, HashMap vs TreeMap.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong collection choice leads to performance issues or subtle bugs. Using a List where you need uniqueness, or a HashMap where you need ordering, causes problems that surface only at scale.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new collection declarations. Is the collection type appropriate for the access pattern? Is ArrayList used for random access? Is LinkedHashMap used when insertion order matters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses a List but checks for duplicates with contains() in a loop. A Set would provide O(1) lookups and enforce uniqueness automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code: Set<String> uniqueIds = new LinkedHashSet<>(allIds); // O(1) lookups, preserves order

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick the collection that matches your access pattern. List for ordered, Set for unique, Map for lookup.

items.text: Are collections returned as unmodifiable views when callers shouldn't modify them?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Methods should return unmodifiable views of internal collections to prevent callers from accidentally or intentionally modifying the object's internal state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning a mutable reference to an internal collection breaks encapsulation. Callers can add, remove, or clear elements, causing bugs that are hard to trace back to the mutation site.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check methods that return collections. Are they wrapped with Collections.unmodifiableList() or List.copyOf()? Or are they returning the internal field directly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This getter returns the internal list directly. A caller could modify it and corrupt this object's state. Consider returning Collections.unmodifiableList() or List.copyOf().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never return mutable references to internal collections. Return unmodifiable views or copies.

items.text: Are raw types avoided (always use `List<String>`, never just `List`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Always use parameterized generic types like List<String> instead of raw types like List. Raw types lose type safety at compile time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Raw types bypass generics entirely, allowing any type to be inserted. This moves type errors from compile time to runtime, where they manifest as ClassCastException in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for raw type usage: unparameterized List, Map, Set, Class, etc. Check for unchecked cast warnings that indicate raw type usage.
baseContent.exampleComment: This method accepts a raw List parameter. Could we parameterize it as List<Order> to get compile-time type safety?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use raw types. Generics exist to catch type errors at compile time.

items.text: Are wildcard generics (`? extends`, `? super`) used correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use ? extends T (upper bound) when you only read from a generic container, and ? super T (lower bound) when you only write to it. This is the PECS principle: Producer Extends, Consumer Super.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wildcard generics make APIs more flexible. A method accepting List<? extends Number> can accept List<Integer>, List<Double>, etc., rather than being locked to a single concrete type.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic method signatures. Are read-only parameters using ? extends? Are write-only parameters using ? super? Is PECS followed correctly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This method only reads from the list but requires List<Number> exactly. Changing to List<? extends Number> would let callers pass List<Integer> or List<Double>.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. Use wildcards to make generic APIs flexible.

items.text: Are empty collections returned instead of `null` (e.g., `Collections.emptyList()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Methods returning collections should return empty collections (Collections.emptyList(), List.of()) instead of null when there are no results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning null forces every caller to add a null check before iterating. Returning an empty collection is safe to iterate over and eliminates NullPointerException risks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check methods that return List, Set, or Map. Do any code paths return null? Could they return an empty collection instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This method returns null when there are no results. Returning Collections.emptyList() would be safer and eliminate null checks at all call sites.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Return empty collections, not null. It eliminates an entire class of NullPointerExceptions.

items.text: Are checked exceptions used for recoverable conditions and unchecked for programming errors?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Checked exceptions should represent recoverable conditions (file not found, network timeout). Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) should represent programming errors (null dereference, illegal argument).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using checked exceptions for everything clutters code with meaningless catch blocks. Using unchecked for everything means recoverable errors are silently ignored. The distinction guides callers on what to handle.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new exception classes. Are checked exceptions used for conditions the caller can reasonably recover from? Are RuntimeExceptions used for bugs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This throws a checked IOException for an invalid argument. Since the caller cannot recover from bad input, consider using IllegalArgumentException (unchecked) instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Checked exceptions for recoverable conditions, unchecked for programming errors.

items.text: Are exceptions caught at the appropriate level (not too broad, not swallowed)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Exceptions should be caught where they can be meaningfully handled, not at every layer. Low-level code should throw; high-level code should catch and decide what to do.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Catching too early hides errors from code that could handle them better. Catching too late lets exceptions escape to users as stack traces. The right level depends on where recovery logic belongs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check catch blocks. Can the catching code actually recover, or is it just logging and rethrowing? Would it be better to let the exception propagate to a higher-level handler?
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block just logs and rethrows. If recovery is not possible here, let the exception propagate to the service layer where we have a proper error handler.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch exceptions where you can actually do something useful. Do not catch-log-rethrow.

items.text: Are catch blocks doing something meaningful (logging, wrapping, rethrowing), not just `e.printStackTrace()`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every catch block should have a purpose: recover, wrap and rethrow with context, translate to an error response, or compensate. Empty catch blocks or swallowed exceptions hide bugs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Swallowed exceptions make systems fail silently. When something goes wrong, no one knows, and the root cause is lost. This makes debugging exponentially harder.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check every catch block in the diff. Is it empty? Does it only log? Does it return null silently? Could it provide a better error response or retry?
baseContent.exampleComment: This empty catch block silently swallows the exception. At minimum, log the error. Better yet, wrap it in a domain exception with additional context.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never swallow exceptions silently. Every catch block should add value.

items.text: Are custom exception classes used where they add semantic value?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom exception classes should be created when they add semantic meaning beyond what standard exceptions provide. Do not create custom exceptions that are just renames of existing ones.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Custom exceptions enable targeted catch blocks, carry domain-specific data (like error codes or entity IDs), and make error handling more precise. But too many exception types add complexity without benefit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new exception classes. Do they carry additional context? Could a standard exception (IllegalArgumentException, IllegalStateException) serve the same purpose?
baseContent.exampleComment: This custom exception is essentially a renamed RuntimeException with no additional data. Could we use IllegalStateException with a descriptive message instead?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Create custom exceptions only when they carry domain-specific data or enable targeted handling.

items.text: Is `finally` or try-with-resources used for resource cleanup (streams, connections, locks)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Resources like streams, connections, and file handles must be closed after use. Java's try-with-resources statement guarantees cleanup even if an exception occurs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked resources cause memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and file descriptor exhaustion. These bugs are hard to reproduce locally but cause outages at scale.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for any resource acquisition (new FileInputStream, getConnection(), etc.). Is it in a try-with-resources block? Are AutoCloseable resources properly closed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This database connection is acquired but not in a try-with-resources block. If the query throws, the connection will leak.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use try-with-resources for AutoCloseable objects. Never rely on manual close calls.

items.text: Are `catch (Exception e)` or `catch (Throwable t)` blocks justified and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Catching Exception or Throwable is overly broad and catches errors you probably cannot handle, like OutOfMemoryError or StackOverflowError.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broad catches hide bugs, swallow unexpected errors, and can mask serious JVM issues. They make it impossible to distinguish recoverable from non-recoverable failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for catch(Exception or catch(Throwable. Is each one justified with a comment? Could a more specific exception type be caught instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This catches Exception broadly. Could we catch the specific exceptions this code can throw (IOException, TimeoutException) and let unexpected errors propagate?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch specific exceptions. Broad catches hide bugs and mask serious errors.

items.text: Are error messages descriptive enough for debugging (include relevant IDs, state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error messages should include enough context to diagnose the issue without needing a debugger: what was expected, what was received, and relevant identifiers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In production, error messages are often all you have. A message like 'Processing failed' tells you nothing. 'Order 12345 processing failed: expected status PENDING but was CANCELLED' tells you everything.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read exception messages and log statements. Could you diagnose the issue from the message alone? Are relevant IDs, expected values, and actual values included?
baseContent.exampleComment: The error message 'Invalid input' does not help debugging. Could we include what field was invalid and what value was received?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write error messages for the on-call engineer at 3 AM. Include IDs, expected values, and actual values.

items.text: Are retries implemented with backoff and bounded attempts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Retry logic should use exponential backoff (increasing delays between attempts) and a maximum attempt count to avoid overwhelming failing services.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Immediate retries without backoff can amplify failures, turning a brief hiccup into a sustained outage. Unbounded retries can run forever. Combined, they create retry storms.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check retry logic. Is there a delay between attempts? Does the delay increase? Is there a maximum attempt count? Consider using a library like Resilience4j or Spring Retry.
baseContent.exampleComment: This retry loop has no backoff and no maximum attempts. If the downstream service is down, this will spin forever. Consider adding exponential backoff with a max of 3-5 attempts.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use bounded retries with exponential backoff. Consider jitter to prevent thundering herds.

items.text: Is `@Nullable` / `@NonNull` annotated consistently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use @Nullable and @NonNull annotations to document nullability contracts. This helps static analysis tools catch null dereferences at compile time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: NullPointerException is one of the most common runtime errors in Java. Nullability annotations make the contract explicit and enable tools like NullAway, Error Prone, and IntelliJ to catch violations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new method parameters and return types. Are @Nullable/@NonNull annotations used consistently? Are tools like NullAway or Checker Framework enabled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This parameter can be null based on the caller, but it is not annotated. Adding @Nullable would make the contract explicit and help static analysis catch misuse.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Annotate nullability explicitly. Let static analysis catch NullPointerExceptions before production does.

items.text: Are shared mutable state accesses properly synchronized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Any mutable state accessed by multiple threads must be protected by proper synchronization mechanisms: synchronized blocks, locks, or concurrent data structures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unsynchronized access to shared mutable state causes data races, leading to corrupted data, lost updates, and nondeterministic behavior that is nearly impossible to reproduce in testing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Identify mutable fields accessed from multiple threads. Are they protected by synchronized, Lock, or wrapped in a concurrent collection? Are all access paths covered?
baseContent.exampleComment: This HashMap is accessed from multiple request threads but is not synchronized. Consider using ConcurrentHashMap or synchronizing access.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If multiple threads touch it and it can change, it must be synchronized.

items.text: Is `ConcurrentHashMap` used instead of `synchronizedMap` where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ConcurrentHashMap provides better concurrent performance than Collections.synchronizedMap() because it uses lock striping instead of a single lock for the entire map.
baseContent.whyItMatters: synchronizedMap locks the entire map for every operation, creating a bottleneck. ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and partitioned writes, offering dramatically better throughput under contention.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for Collections.synchronizedMap or Hashtable usage. Could ConcurrentHashMap be used instead? Are compound operations (check-then-act) done atomically?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses Collections.synchronizedMap but the check-then-put is not atomic. ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent would handle this atomically and with better performance.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent access. Use its atomic methods for compound operations.

items.text: Are `volatile` fields used correctly for visibility guarantees?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The volatile keyword ensures that reads and writes to a field are visible across threads. It does not provide atomicity for compound operations like increment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without volatile, the JVM may cache field values in CPU registers or reorder instructions, causing one thread to see stale values written by another. Volatile prevents this for simple read/write operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check fields accessed by multiple threads. Are simple flag fields marked volatile? Are compound operations (read-modify-write) using AtomicInteger or synchronized instead of volatile?
baseContent.exampleComment: This 'running' flag is read by multiple threads but is not volatile. Other threads may never see the update. Mark it volatile or use AtomicBoolean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use volatile for simple flags shared between threads. For compound operations, use Atomic* classes.

items.text: Are `AtomicInteger`, `AtomicReference`, etc. preferred over `synchronized` for simple counters?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Java's java.util.concurrent.atomic classes provide lock-free thread-safe operations for single variables. They are preferred over synchronized blocks for simple atomic operations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Atomic classes use CPU-level compare-and-swap (CAS) operations, which are faster than acquiring and releasing locks. They also eliminate the possibility of deadlocks for single-variable operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for synchronized blocks that protect a single counter or reference. Could they be replaced with AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, or AtomicReference?
baseContent.exampleComment: This synchronized block just increments a counter. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() would be simpler and faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Atomic* classes for single-variable thread safety. Reserve synchronized for multi-variable invariants.

items.text: Are thread pools properly sized and named (for debugging)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Thread pools should be appropriately sized for their workload (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound) and threads should have descriptive names for debugging.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An undersized pool causes starvation and queuing. An oversized pool wastes memory and causes excessive context switching. Unnamed threads make thread dumps unreadable during incident response.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check thread pool creation. Is the pool size justified? Are threads named with a meaningful prefix? Is a custom ThreadFactory used for naming?
baseContent.exampleComment: This thread pool uses Executors.newCachedThreadPool() which is unbounded. For this I/O-heavy workload, a fixed pool of ~2x CPU cores with named threads would be safer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Unbounded pool, unnamed threads
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newCachedThreadPool();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
ExecutorService executor = new ThreadPoolExecutor(
    4, 16,
    60L, TimeUnit.SECONDS,
    new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(1000),
    new ThreadFactoryBuilder()
        .setNameFormat("order-processor-%d")
        .setDaemon(true)
        .build(),
    new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy());

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Size pools for the workload, name threads for debuggability, and always set a rejection policy.

items.text: Are `ExecutorService` instances shut down properly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ExecutorService instances must be shut down when no longer needed to prevent thread leaks. Use shutdown() for graceful termination and shutdownNow() for immediate termination.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An ExecutorService that is never shut down keeps its threads alive, preventing JVM shutdown and leaking resources. In application servers, this causes thread leaks across deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that every ExecutorService has a corresponding shutdown call, preferably in a finally block, @PreDestroy method, or shutdown hook. Are in-progress tasks given time to complete?
baseContent.exampleComment: This ExecutorService is created but never shut down. If this bean is destroyed during application shutdown, threads will leak. Add a @PreDestroy method to shut it down.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always shut down ExecutorService instances. Give in-progress tasks time to complete gracefully.

items.text: Is there risk of deadlock (lock ordering issues)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deadlock occurs when two or more threads hold locks and each waits for a lock held by another. Consistent lock ordering and timeout-based locking prevent this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deadlocks freeze threads permanently. They are hard to reproduce, hard to diagnose without thread dumps, and can bring entire services down under specific timing conditions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for code that acquires multiple locks. Is the ordering consistent across all call paths? Could tryLock with a timeout be used instead of blocking acquisition?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code acquires lockA then lockB, but another method acquires lockB then lockA. This creates a deadlock risk. Could we enforce a consistent lock ordering?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always acquire locks in a consistent, well-defined order. Use tryLock with timeouts when possible.

items.text: Are `CompletableFuture` chains handling exceptions with `exceptionally()` or `handle()`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CompletableFuture chains must handle exceptions at each stage or at the end. Unhandled exceptions in async chains are silently swallowed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unlike synchronous code where exceptions propagate naturally, CompletableFuture swallows exceptions by default. If you do not add exception handling, failures are invisible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CompletableFuture chains. Do they have exceptionally(), handle(), or whenComplete() stages? Are exceptions from async operations being logged or translated?
baseContent.exampleComment: This CompletableFuture chain has no exception handling. If any stage fails, the error will be silently swallowed. Add an exceptionally() or handle() stage.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always add exception handling to CompletableFuture chains. Unhandled async exceptions vanish silently.

items.text: Are race conditions possible in read-modify-write sequences?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A read-modify-write sequence (check a value, compute a new value, write it back) is not atomic unless explicitly synchronized. Another thread can intervene between the read and write.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Race conditions cause lost updates, double processing, and inconsistent state. They are timing-dependent, making them rare in testing but common in production under load.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for patterns where a value is read, modified, and written back without synchronization. Check-then-act patterns (if not exists, then create) are classic race conditions.
baseContent.exampleComment: The check-then-put on this map is a race condition. Between containsKey() and put(), another thread could insert the same key. Use computeIfAbsent() for atomicity.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// Atomic operation - no race condition
cache.computeIfAbsent(key, this::expensiveCompute);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Read-modify-write must be atomic. Use concurrent data structure methods or explicit synchronization.

items.text: Are field numbers stable and never reused for different semantics?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Protobuf field numbers are part of the wire format. Once assigned, a field number must never be changed or reused for a different field, even after the original field is removed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Field numbers are how protobuf identifies fields on the wire. Reusing a number means old data will be deserialized into the wrong field, causing silent data corruption that is extremely hard to diagnose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that no existing field numbers have been changed. If fields were removed, verify their numbers are reserved. New fields should use the next available number.
baseContent.exampleComment: Field number 3 was previously used for 'legacy_status'. Reusing it for 'priority' will cause existing serialized data to deserialize incorrectly. Please use the next available number.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Field numbers are forever. Never reuse them. Reserve removed field numbers.

items.text: Are deprecated fields marked with `reserved` to prevent reuse?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When removing a field from a proto message, mark both the field number and field name as reserved to prevent accidental reuse in the future.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without reserved, a future developer might unknowingly reuse the field number or name, causing wire incompatibility with existing serialized data or old clients.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for removed fields. Are the field numbers and names added to the reserved statement? Is there a comment explaining what the field was?
baseContent.exampleComment: This field removal does not include a reserved statement. Future developers might reuse field number 5, causing data corruption. Please add reserved 5 and reserved 'old_field_name'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always reserve removed field numbers and names. It is a safety net for future development.

items.text: Are field names in `snake_case` as per protobuf convention?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Protobuf field names should use lower_snake_case as per the official style guide. The protobuf compiler generates appropriate casing for each target language.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming ensures generated code follows each language's conventions automatically. Java gets camelCase getters, Python gets snake_case - but only if the proto uses snake_case.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new field names in .proto files. Are they in lower_snake_case? Are there any camelCase or UPPER_CASE field names?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field 'userId' should be 'user_id' per protobuf style conventions. The protobuf compiler will generate the appropriate getUserId() getter in Java.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use lower_snake_case for proto field names. Let the compiler handle language-specific casing.

items.text: Are enums used with an `UNSPECIFIED = 0` default value?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every proto3 enum should have an UNSPECIFIED = 0 value as its first entry. This serves as the default when no value is explicitly set.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In proto3, unset enum fields default to 0. Without an UNSPECIFIED value at 0, the default silently maps to a real business value, which can cause incorrect behavior when clients forget to set the field.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check enum definitions. Does each one have a _UNSPECIFIED = 0 value? Is the 0 value truly a sentinel and not a valid business state?
baseContent.exampleComment: This enum starts with ACTIVE = 0. If a client does not set the status field, it will default to ACTIVE silently. Add STATUS_UNSPECIFIED = 0 as the first value.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use UNSPECIFIED = 0 as the default enum value. It makes unset fields detectable.

items.text: Are `oneof` fields used where exactly one of several fields should be set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use oneof when a message should contain exactly one of several mutually exclusive fields. This makes the constraint explicit in the schema.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without oneof, nothing prevents setting multiple mutually exclusive fields simultaneously. Oneof enforces mutual exclusivity at the wire level and generates type-safe accessor code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for groups of fields where only one should be set at a time. Could they be modeled as a oneof? Are existing oneof fields being used correctly?
baseContent.exampleComment: The payment_method fields (card_number, bank_account, wallet_id) are mutually exclusive. Modeling them as a oneof would enforce this constraint at the schema level.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use oneof for mutually exclusive fields. It encodes business constraints in the schema.

items.text: Are nested message types used to scope closely related messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use nested message types to scope related messages within their parent, avoiding namespace pollution and making relationships explicit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flat proto files with dozens of top-level messages become hard to navigate. Nesting communicates that a message is only used within the context of its parent, reducing cognitive overhead.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new message types. Are there messages that are only used as fields of one parent message? Could they be nested inside the parent?
baseContent.exampleComment: The OrderItem message is only used inside Order. Nesting it as Order.Item would make the relationship explicit and reduce top-level clutter.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Nest message types that belong exclusively to a parent. It communicates scope and ownership.

items.text: Are `repeated` fields used instead of separate count + values patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use repeated fields for lists of items instead of numbered fields like item_1, item_2, item_3. Repeated fields are the protobuf way to represent arrays.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Numbered fields create a fixed maximum, require schema changes to grow, and generate verbose code. Repeated fields are flexible, efficient, and idiomatic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for patterns of numbered fields that represent a collection. Could they be consolidated into a single repeated field?
baseContent.exampleComment: The fields tag_1 through tag_5 should be a single repeated string tags field. This allows any number of tags without schema changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use repeated fields for collections. Avoid numbered field patterns like field_1, field_2.

items.text: Are `google.protobuf.Timestamp` and `google.protobuf.Duration` used instead of raw integers for time?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use google.protobuf.Timestamp for points in time and google.protobuf.Duration for time spans, rather than custom int64 or string fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Well-known types provide standardized semantics, cross-language conversion utilities, and JSON serialization support. Custom timestamp fields require documentation and manual conversion in every language.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for int64 or string fields that represent timestamps or durations. Could they use the well-known types instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field 'created_at_millis' as int64 would be better as google.protobuf.Timestamp. It provides standardized semantics and automatic conversion to Java Instant.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use well-known types for timestamps and durations. They provide standardized semantics across languages.

items.text: Are `google.protobuf.FieldMask` used for partial update APIs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use google.protobuf.FieldMask in update requests to specify which fields should be modified, enabling partial updates without overwriting unset fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without FieldMask, update operations must either send the entire object (risk overwriting concurrent changes) or add a separate boolean for each field (verbose and error-prone).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check update RPCs. Do they use FieldMask to specify which fields to update? Are unset fields preserved rather than zeroed out?
baseContent.exampleComment: This UpdateUser RPC takes the full User message but cannot distinguish between 'set name to empty string' and 'do not change name'. Adding a FieldMask solves this.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use FieldMask for partial updates. It makes intent explicit and prevents accidental overwrites.

items.text: Are `bytes` fields used for opaque binary data, not `string`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use bytes fields for opaque binary data (files, encrypted content, hashes) and string fields for human-readable text. String fields must be valid UTF-8 in proto3.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Putting binary data in a string field causes UTF-8 validation failures, silent data corruption, and serialization errors. Conversely, using bytes for text loses the UTF-8 guarantee.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new fields. Are binary payloads stored in bytes fields? Are text fields using string? Is base64 encoding used unnecessarily?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'file_content' field is declared as string but stores binary data. This should be bytes to avoid UTF-8 encoding issues.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use bytes for binary data, string for text. They have different encoding guarantees on the wire.

items.text: Is the change backward compatible? Can old readers still parse new messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Backward compatibility means old clients can still read messages written by new code. New fields should have safe defaults, and removed fields should be reserved.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In distributed systems, not all services upgrade simultaneously. If a new message version cannot be read by old clients, rolling deployments will fail and require a coordinated big-bang release.
baseContent.howToVerify: Would an old client reading a new message get sensible behavior? Are new fields optional with safe defaults? Are removed fields reserved?
baseContent.exampleComment: Adding this required field breaks backward compatibility. Old clients will not set it, and new code that expects it will fail. Consider making it optional with a default behavior.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: New fields must be optional with safe defaults. Always think about what old clients will see.

items.text: Is the change forward compatible? Can new readers still parse old messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Forward compatibility means new clients can read messages written by old code. New code should handle the absence of new fields gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When rolling out changes, new code will receive messages from old producers that lack new fields. If new code crashes or behaves incorrectly on missing fields, the rollout fails.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new code handle messages without the new fields? Are there null checks or default values for new fields? Can the system function if the new field is never set?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new code assumes the 'priority' field is always set, but old producers will not include it. Add a default value check: if unset, treat as NORMAL priority.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: New code must work correctly with old messages. Handle missing fields with sensible defaults.

items.text: Are required fields avoided (proto3 doesn't support them, proto2 they're dangerous)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto3 removed the required keyword. In proto2, required fields should be avoided because they make schema evolution dangerous.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Required fields cannot be removed without breaking all existing clients. They make it impossible to evolve the schema gradually, forcing big-bang migrations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for 'required' keyword in proto2 files. In proto3, check that code does not treat fields as implicitly required by crashing when they are absent.
baseContent.exampleComment: This proto2 message uses required fields. If we ever need to remove them, all clients must update simultaneously. Consider migrating to proto3 optional fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid required fields. They create permanent contracts that cannot be safely evolved.

items.text: Are field numbers not changed for existing fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Existing field numbers must never be changed. The field number is how protobuf identifies fields on the wire, not the field name.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Changing a field number is equivalent to deleting the old field and creating a new one. All existing serialized data becomes unreadable for that field, causing silent data loss.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare the proto diff carefully. Has any existing field's number changed? Even a swap between two fields is a breaking change.
baseContent.exampleComment: This diff changes the field number of 'customer_id' from 2 to 5. This is a wire-breaking change - all existing serialized data will lose this field. Please revert the field number.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Field numbers are permanent. Changing one is a wire-breaking change that corrupts data.

items.text: Are field types not changed in incompatible ways (e.g., `int32` to `string`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changing a field's type can break wire compatibility. Some changes are safe (int32 to int64), but many are not (string to int32, message to enum).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incompatible type changes cause deserialization errors or silent data corruption. A string field changed to int32 will fail to parse existing serialized data entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for field type changes in the proto diff. Consult the protobuf compatibility table for safe type changes. When in doubt, add a new field instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: Changing 'amount' from string to int64 is a wire-breaking change. Existing serialized data has this as a string. Consider adding a new field 'amount_cents' instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Do not change field types. Add a new field and deprecate the old one.

items.text: If a field is removed, is the field number `reserved`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When removing a field from a proto message, use the reserved keyword for both the field number and name to prevent future reuse.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without reserved, a future developer might accidentally assign the same field number to a new field, causing deserialization of old data into the wrong field type - silent data corruption.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check proto diffs for removed fields. Is the field number added to a reserved statement? Is the field name also reserved?
baseContent.exampleComment: Field 4 (legacy_notes) was removed but not reserved. Please add 'reserved 4; reserved "legacy_notes";' to prevent future reuse.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reserve removed field numbers and names. It prevents accidental reuse and data corruption.

items.text: Is the `.proto` file's `package` declaration correct and consistent?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto files should have a package declaration that matches the directory structure and follows the organization's naming convention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The package prevents naming collisions between message types and affects the generated Java package. An incorrect package can cause compilation errors or put generated code in the wrong location.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that the package matches the directory path. Verify it follows the convention (e.g., com.company.service.v1). Check java_package option if different from the proto package.
baseContent.exampleComment: The proto package is 'order' but this file is in the 'com/company/order/v1/' directory. The package should be 'com.company.order.v1' to match.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Proto package declarations should match directory structure and set java_package explicitly.

items.text: Are `import` statements using the correct paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto import paths should be correct and use the appropriate import style. Use relative paths from the proto root, not absolute filesystem paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect import paths cause compilation failures. Using the wrong import style can break builds in other environments or when proto files are moved.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import statements in proto files. Do they resolve correctly? Are they using paths relative to the proto root? Are public imports used only when intentional?
baseContent.exampleComment: This import uses an absolute path that only works on your machine. Change it to a path relative to the proto root directory.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use proto root-relative import paths. They work consistently across environments.

items.text: Is the generated Java code used correctly (using builders, not constructing with `new`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto-generated Java classes are immutable. Use the Builder pattern to create and modify messages. Never try to set fields directly on a built message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Generated proto messages are immutable by design. Misusing the generated API leads to compilation errors, runtime exceptions, or incorrect patterns like creating messages without builders.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that proto messages are created using the builder pattern. Are toBuilder/build calls used for modifications? Is the generated API used idiomatically?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code creates a proto message by calling new Order() directly. Proto messages should be created using Order.newBuilder().setId(...).build().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
Order order = Order.newBuilder()
    .setId("123")
    .setCustomerId("cust-456")
    .setStatus(OrderStatus.ORDER_STATUS_ACTIVE)
    .build();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use builders to create proto messages. Generated messages are immutable.

items.text: Are `.toBuilder()` and `.build()` patterns used for modifications?

baseContent.whatItMeans: To modify an existing proto message, use toBuilder() to get a builder with the current values, modify it, and call build() to get a new immutable message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Since proto messages are immutable, the toBuilder/build pattern is the only safe way to create a modified copy. Alternatives like reflection or serialization tricks are fragile and defeat type safety.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for proto message modifications. Is toBuilder() used to create modified copies? Are there anti-patterns like building a new message from scratch when a copy-and-modify would be clearer?
baseContent.exampleComment: Instead of building a new Order from scratch, use existingOrder.toBuilder().setStatus(COMPLETED).build() to preserve all other fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use toBuilder() to modify existing messages. It preserves all fields you do not explicitly change.

items.text: Are `hasField()` checks used before accessing optional fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use hasField() to check if a message field is set before accessing it. In proto3, unset message fields return a default instance, which may not be distinguishable from an intentionally empty message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without hasField(), you cannot distinguish between 'field was explicitly set to default value' and 'field was never set'. This matters for optional fields and nested messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check accesses to optional and message-type fields. Are hasField() checks used where the distinction between unset and default matters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code accesses order.getShippingAddress() without checking hasShippingAddress() first. For orders without shipping (like digital products), this returns a default Address, not null.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use hasField() for message-type fields to distinguish between unset and default instances.

items.text: Is `.getDefaultInstance()` used instead of `null` for absent messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Proto.getDefaultInstance() instead of null when you need an empty or absent proto message. Proto APIs are designed around default instances, not null.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Passing null where a proto message is expected causes NullPointerExceptions. The default instance is a valid, empty message that works safely with all proto operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for null checks on proto message parameters. Could getDefaultInstance() be used instead? Are there places where null proto messages are passed or returned?
baseContent.exampleComment: This method returns null when there is no order. Return Order.getDefaultInstance() instead - it is safer and works with all proto operations like merging.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use getDefaultInstance() instead of null for proto messages. It eliminates NullPointerExceptions.

items.text: Are proto messages not used as map keys (they're mutable)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto messages should not be used as keys in HashMaps or elements in HashSets. Their equals/hashCode behavior may not match expectations and can change between proto library versions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto message equality is complex (unknown fields, default values, float precision). Using them as map keys leads to subtle bugs where lookups fail even for logically equivalent messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for proto types used as Map keys or Set elements. Extract a meaningful key (like the ID field) instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using the full Order proto as a map key is fragile. Extract order.getId() as the key instead - it is stable, predictable, and efficient.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
Map<String, List<Shipment>> orderShipments = new HashMap<>();
orderShipments.put(order.getId(), shipments);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use proto messages as map keys. Extract a stable identifier field instead.

items.text: Is serialization/deserialization done with the correct methods (`toByteArray()`, `parseFrom()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the correct serialization methods: toByteArray()/parseFrom() for binary, JsonFormat for JSON. Do not use Java serialization (Serializable) on proto messages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto's binary format is compact, fast, and wire-compatible. Java serialization is slow, bloated, and not compatible across proto library versions. Using the wrong method defeats proto's advantages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check how proto messages are serialized. Is toByteArray()/parseFrom() used for binary? Is JsonFormat used for JSON? Is Java's ObjectOutputStream being used accidentally?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code serializes the proto using ObjectOutputStream. Use order.toByteArray() for binary or JsonFormat.printer() for JSON instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use proto's own serialization methods. Never use Java serialization for proto messages.

items.text: Are RPC methods named with verbs describing the action (`CreateUser`, `ListOrders`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RPC method names should use verbs that describe the action: CreateOrder, GetUser, ListProducts, UpdateConfig, DeleteSession.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent verb-based naming makes APIs intuitive and predictable. Developers can guess method names without reading docs. It also aligns with Google's API design guidelines.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check RPC method names. Do they start with standard verbs (Get, List, Create, Update, Delete, Batch)? Are custom methods using descriptive verb phrases?
baseContent.exampleComment: The RPC 'OrderSubmission' should be named 'SubmitOrder' using a verb-first pattern. This aligns with the standard API naming convention.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name RPCs with verbs: Create, Get, List, Update, Delete. The name is the action.

items.text: Are request/response messages appropriately structured and not overly generic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each RPC should have its own dedicated request and response message types, even if they seem similar to other RPCs. Do not reuse generic request/response types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dedicated message types allow each RPC to evolve independently. Shared types create coupling - adding a field for one RPC leaks it to all others that share the type.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each RPC has its own request/response types named after the RPC (e.g., CreateOrderRequest/CreateOrderResponse). Are any types shared across unrelated RPCs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The GenericRequest type is shared across three RPCs. Each should have its own request type so they can evolve independently.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One request/response type per RPC. Dedicated types enable independent evolution.

items.text: Are pagination fields included for list endpoints (`page_token`, `page_size`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List RPCs should include pagination support with page_size and page_token fields in the request, and next_page_token in the response.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without pagination, list endpoints return unbounded results that can overwhelm memory, timeout, or crash the service. Even if the dataset is small today, it may grow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check List RPCs. Do they have page_size and page_token in the request? Is next_page_token in the response? Is there a maximum page_size enforced server-side?
baseContent.exampleComment: The ListOrders RPC has no pagination fields. Even if the result set is small now, adding page_size and page_token future-proofs the API.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always include pagination in List RPCs. Data grows, and unbounded lists eventually break.

items.text: Are idempotency keys used for mutating operations where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mutating operations (Create, Update, Delete) should support idempotency keys so that retried requests do not create duplicate resources or apply changes twice.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Network failures and retries are inevitable. Without idempotency, a retried CreateOrder could create two orders. Idempotency keys let the server detect and deduplicate retries safely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mutating RPCs. Do Create requests include a request_id or idempotency_key field? Is the server deduplicating based on this key?
baseContent.exampleComment: The CreatePayment RPC has no idempotency key. If a client retries after a timeout, it could charge the customer twice. Add a request_id field for deduplication.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add idempotency keys to mutating operations. Retries must be safe.

items.text: Are error codes/status codes used correctly (gRPC status codes, HTTP status codes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC status codes (OK, NOT_FOUND, INVALID_ARGUMENT, etc.) and HTTP status codes should be used correctly and consistently to communicate the nature of errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Correct status codes enable proper error handling by clients, monitoring, and alerting. Using INTERNAL for everything hides the distinction between client errors and server errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling in RPC implementations. Are the right gRPC status codes used? Is NOT_FOUND used for missing resources? INVALID_ARGUMENT for bad input? INTERNAL only for unexpected server errors?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns INTERNAL status for invalid input. Use INVALID_ARGUMENT (or 400 Bad Request) so the client knows it is their error and does not retry.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use specific status codes. INVALID_ARGUMENT for bad input, NOT_FOUND for missing resources, INTERNAL only for bugs.

items.text: Are API changes documented in relevant API docs or changelogs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes should be reflected in API documentation, changelogs, or migration guides so that consumers know about new fields, changed behavior, or deprecations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented API changes surprise consumers and erode trust. Documentation is the contract between the API provider and its consumers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR includes documentation updates. Are new fields documented with comments in the proto file? Is there a changelog entry? Are breaking changes called out?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new 'priority' field to the CreateOrder RPC but the API docs are not updated. Consumers will not know about this new capability.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document API changes in proto comments and changelogs. Consumers depend on your documentation.

items.text: Are timeouts and deadlines set appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RPC calls should have explicit timeouts/deadlines. Without them, a hung downstream service can hold connections indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing timeouts turn partial failures into total failures. A single hung RPC can exhaust thread pools and cascade through the entire system. Timeouts are circuit breakers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check gRPC client configurations. Are deadlines set for outgoing calls? Are server-side timeouts configured? Are timeouts propagated across service boundaries?
baseContent.exampleComment: This gRPC call has no deadline set. If the downstream service hangs, this thread will block indefinitely. Add a deadline of 5-10 seconds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// No timeout - can hang forever
OrderResponse response = orderStub.createOrder(request);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set deadlines on RPC calls. No timeout means infinite wait on failure.

items.text: Is input validation done early and comprehensively?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate all input at the API boundary before processing. Check for required fields, valid ranges, format constraints, and business rules.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Early validation provides clear error messages to callers and prevents invalid data from propagating through the system. Late validation causes confusing errors deep in business logic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check RPC handler methods. Is input validated before business logic runs? Are validation errors returned with specific field information? Are all edge cases covered?
baseContent.exampleComment: This handler processes the order before validating the input. If customer_id is empty, the error comes from the database layer instead of a clear INVALID_ARGUMENT response.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate early, fail fast. Clear validation errors at the boundary save debugging time.

items.text: Does the change respect module/package boundaries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changes should respect the defined module and package boundaries. Code in module A should not directly access internal classes of module B.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Violating module boundaries creates tight coupling between independently deployable units. This makes it impossible to refactor, version, or deploy modules independently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the change imports internal classes from other modules. Is it using the module's public API? Are package-private classes being accessed from outside their package?
baseContent.exampleComment: This class imports an internal implementation class from the payment module. Could we use the PaymentService public API instead to maintain module boundaries?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Depend on module APIs, not internal implementations. Module boundaries exist for a reason.

items.text: Are cross-module dependencies introduced intentionally and reviewed by owning teams?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New dependencies between modules should be intentional and reviewed carefully. Accidental dependencies increase coupling and make the dependency graph harder to manage.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every cross-module dependency is a coupling point. Circular dependencies, diamond dependencies, and excessive fan-out all make the system harder to build, test, and deploy independently.
