baseContent.howToVerify: Check build file changes (pom.xml, build.gradle). Are new module dependencies added? Are they necessary? Could the functionality be achieved without the new dependency?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a dependency from order-service to notification-service. Was this intentional? Could we use an event/message to decouple these modules instead?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cross-module dependencies should be deliberate. Prefer events and interfaces over direct coupling.

items.text: Is the build still fast? Are unnecessary dependencies added to the build graph?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changes should not significantly slow down the build. Large generated files, unnecessary recompilation triggers, or heavy new dependencies can degrade build times.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Slow builds reduce developer productivity and increase CI costs. In a monorepo, a single change that slows the build affects every developer on the team.
baseContent.howToVerify: Did the change add heavy new dependencies? Are proto files generating excessive code? Is incremental compilation still working? Are build cache keys stable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a dependency on a large framework that will significantly increase compile time. Could we use a lighter alternative or extract just the functionality we need?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Guard build performance. Every dependency and generated file adds to build time.

items.text: Are shared libraries versioned or pinned appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Shared libraries in a monorepo should have clear versioning. All modules should use compatible versions to avoid dependency conflicts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Version mismatches cause ClassNotFoundException, NoSuchMethodError, and other runtime errors that are hard to diagnose. Consistent versions prevent 'works on my module' syndrome.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for version changes in dependency declarations. Are shared library versions managed centrally? Are there conflicting versions across modules?
baseContent.exampleComment: This module pins protobuf-java to 3.21.0 but other modules use 3.25.0. This version mismatch could cause runtime errors. Use the version from the shared BOM.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Manage shared library versions centrally. Inconsistent versions cause hard-to-diagnose runtime errors.

items.text: Does the change affect other modules' builds or tests unexpectedly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changes to shared code, proto definitions, or build configuration can break other modules. Consider the blast radius of your change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In a monorepo, a proto schema change can break dozens of downstream consumers. Understanding the impact before merging prevents widespread CI failures and emergency reverts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Did the change modify shared proto files, common libraries, or build plugins? Check which modules depend on the changed code. Run tests for affected downstream modules.
baseContent.exampleComment: This proto change affects the Order message used by 12 downstream modules. Have we verified that all consumers handle the new field correctly?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Consider the blast radius. Changes to shared code affect every consumer.

items.text: Are internal vs. public APIs clearly separated (`internal` packages, `@VisibleForTesting`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Module APIs should clearly distinguish between public contracts (for other modules) and internal implementation details. Use packages, visibility modifiers, and module systems.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without clear separation, internal details become accidental APIs that other teams depend on. This prevents refactoring and creates invisible coupling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new public classes. Should they be public? Is there a clear api/ package for public types and internal/ for implementation? Are module-info.java exports used?
baseContent.exampleComment: The PaymentProcessor class is public but seems like an internal implementation detail. Could we make it package-private and expose functionality through the PaymentService interface?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Separate public API from internal implementation. Only expose what consumers need.

items.text: Is the change in the correct module, or should it be in a shared library?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code should live in the module that owns its domain. Payment logic belongs in the payment module, not scattered across order-service or user-service.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misplaced code creates implicit dependencies and makes it hard to find functionality. It also prevents teams from owning their domain boundaries cleanly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new code belong in the module it is being added to? Could it be better placed in a different module that owns this domain? Would another team expect to find this code here?
baseContent.exampleComment: This payment validation logic is being added to the order-service module. Should it live in the payment-service module instead, where the rest of the payment logic is?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put code where it belongs. Follow domain ownership boundaries.

items.text: Are generated files checked in or generated at build time consistently with repo conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generated code (proto-generated Java, code generators) should follow the project convention: either checked into source control or generated at build time, but not a mix.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing conventions causes confusion and merge conflicts. Checked-in generated code gets stale. Build-time generation adds build complexity. Pick one approach and be consistent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are generated proto files included in the diff? Does the project convention say they should be generated at build time? Is the build configured to generate them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR checks in generated proto Java files, but our convention is to generate them at build time. Please remove the generated files and ensure the build generates them.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow the project convention for generated files. Consistency prevents confusion and merge conflicts.

items.text: Are there unit tests for new logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New business logic, utility methods, and service classes should have corresponding unit tests that verify correct behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code is a liability. Tests catch regressions, document expected behavior, and give confidence to refactor. Code without tests tends to rot faster.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the test directory for new test files matching the new source files. Are the important code paths covered? Are happy paths and error paths both tested?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new OrderValidator class has no tests. Could you add unit tests covering the validation rules, especially the edge cases around zero quantities and negative prices?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every new code path deserves a test. Tests are documentation that verifies itself.

items.text: Are tests testing behavior, not implementation details?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should verify what the code does (behavior), not how it does it (implementation). Test outputs given inputs, not internal method calls or field values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests coupled to implementation break on every refactor, even when behavior is unchanged. Behavior-focused tests are stable and actually catch regressions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are tests asserting on return values and side effects? Or are they verifying internal state, private method calls, and exact interaction counts?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test verifies that the internal cache was called exactly twice. If we change the caching strategy, the test breaks even though the behavior is the same. Test the output instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test what, not how. If a refactor breaks your test but not the behavior, the test is wrong.

items.text: Are edge cases covered (null inputs, empty collections, boundary values)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should cover edge cases: null inputs, empty collections, boundary values, zero, negative numbers, maximum values, and unicode characters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Edge cases are where most bugs hide. The happy path usually works, but null inputs, empty lists, and boundary values reveal incorrect assumptions and missing validation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test coverage of edge cases. Are null inputs tested? Empty collections? Boundary values? What happens with very large inputs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The tests only cover the happy path. Could you add cases for empty item lists, null customer ID, and orders with zero-quantity items?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test the edges: null, empty, zero, one, many, boundary values. That is where the bugs are.

items.text: Are test names descriptive (`shouldReturnEmptyListWhenNoUsersExist`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test method names should describe the scenario and expected outcome: methodUnderTest_scenario_expectedBehavior. They should read like documentation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails in CI, the name is the first thing you see. A name like 'test1' tells you nothing. A name like 'createOrder_withDuplicateId_throwsConflict' tells you exactly what broke.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the test names. Can you understand what each test verifies without reading the body? Do they follow a consistent naming convention?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test name 'testProcess' does not describe the scenario or expectation. Could you rename it to something like 'processOrder_withExpiredItem_returnsValidationError'?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test names are documentation. method_scenario_expectation tells you everything at a glance.

items.text: Are mocks used appropriately (not over-mocking, not mocking value objects)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mock external dependencies (databases, HTTP clients, third-party services) but do not mock the class under test or its value objects. Over-mocking creates brittle tests that test nothing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When everything is mocked, you are testing mock behavior, not real behavior. Tests pass but production breaks. Mock boundaries, not internals.
baseContent.howToVerify: Count the mocks in each test. Are there more mocks than real objects? Is the class under test being mocked? Are value objects mocked instead of constructed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mocks the Order object that is being tested. Create a real Order using the builder instead. Only mock the OrderRepository (external dependency).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock boundaries, not internals. Real objects give more confidence than mocked ones.

items.text: Are integration tests added for cross-module or database interactions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changes that involve cross-module interactions, database queries, or external service calls should have integration tests that verify the components work together.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unit tests verify individual components, but integration tests verify the wiring. Bugs often hide at boundaries: wrong serialization, incorrect SQL, misconfigured timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the change involve cross-module communication, database access, or gRPC calls? Are there integration tests that exercise these boundaries?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new gRPC endpoint has unit tests but no integration test. Could you add a test that starts the server, sends a real gRPC request, and verifies the response?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unit tests verify components. Integration tests verify they work together. You need both.

items.text: Are proto serialization round-trip tests included for schema changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto messages should have round-trip tests: serialize to bytes, deserialize back, and verify the result equals the original. This catches schema issues early.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Schema changes can silently alter serialization behavior. Round-trip tests verify that your messages survive the wire format correctly and that no data is lost in translation.
baseContent.howToVerify: For new or modified proto messages, are there tests that serialize and deserialize the message? Do they verify all fields survive the round trip?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new proto message does not have a round-trip test. Adding one would catch any serialization issues before they hit production.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Round-trip tests catch serialization bugs before production. Serialize, deserialize, compare.

items.text: Are flaky test patterns avoided (no `Thread.sleep()`, no reliance on ordering)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should be deterministic. Avoid relying on real time, network calls, random data, test execution order, or shared mutable state between tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flaky tests erode trust in the test suite. When tests fail randomly, developers ignore failures, and real bugs slip through. One flaky test poisons the entire suite.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for common flaky patterns: Thread.sleep, real HTTP calls, Date/time dependencies, shared static state, non-deterministic ordering assertions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses Thread.sleep(1000) to wait for an async operation. This is flaky - use CountDownLatch, Awaitility, or a CompletableFuture instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tests must be deterministic. Replace sleep with explicit synchronization mechanisms.

items.text: Is test data constructed with builders/factories, not long constructors?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test data should be created using builders, factory methods, or test fixtures that make the important fields obvious and hide irrelevant boilerplate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests cluttered with data construction are hard to read. The reader cannot tell which fields matter for the test and which are just required boilerplate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test data construction. Are there shared builder helpers or factory methods? Can you tell at a glance which fields are relevant to each test?
baseContent.exampleComment: Each test creates an Order from scratch with 10+ fields. Could we create a test helper like createDefaultOrder() and only override the fields relevant to each test?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use test builders and factories to highlight what matters and hide boilerplate.

items.text: Are assertions specific (`assertEquals` > `assertTrue`, AssertJ > Hamcrest)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Assertions should verify specific values, not just that something is non-null or non-empty. Vague assertions pass when they should fail.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An assertion like assertNotNull(result) passes even if the result contains completely wrong data. Specific assertions catch actual bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check assertions in tests. Are they verifying specific values? Or are they using vague checks like assertNotNull, assertTrue(result != null)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The assertion assertNotNull(order) does not verify the order is correct. Could we assert on the specific fields: ID, status, and total?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be specific in assertions. Test the exact values, not just their existence.

items.text: Are `@ParameterizedTest` or data-driven tests used for repetitive test cases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When testing the same logic with multiple inputs, use @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource, @CsvSource, or @MethodSource instead of duplicating test methods.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated test methods with only different data values add maintenance burden and hide the pattern. Parameterized tests are concise, easy to extend, and clearly show the test matrix.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for test methods that are copy-pasted with only different input values. Could they be consolidated into a parameterized test?
baseContent.exampleComment: These four test methods are identical except for the input status value. A @ParameterizedTest with @EnumSource would be cleaner and easier to extend.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use parameterized tests for multiple inputs. They are concise, extensible, and show the test matrix clearly.

items.text: Are test fixtures and shared setup in `@BeforeEach`, not in each test method?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Common test setup (creating mocks, initializing services, building test data) should be in @BeforeEach methods or shared fixtures, not duplicated in every test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated setup is a maintenance burden and obscures what each test actually verifies. Shared setup makes tests shorter and highlights the unique aspect of each test case.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for repeated setup code across tests. Could it be extracted to @BeforeEach? Is @BeforeAll used only for truly expensive, immutable setup?
baseContent.exampleComment: Each test creates the same mock objects and initializes the service. This setup belongs in a @BeforeEach method so tests can focus on what is unique.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract common setup to @BeforeEach. Tests should focus on what is unique, not shared boilerplate.

items.text: Are database queries efficient? Are N+1 query patterns avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database queries should be optimized: avoid N+1 queries, use batch operations, select only needed columns, and ensure proper WHERE clauses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inefficient queries are the most common performance bottleneck in Java services. A query that works fine for 100 rows can bring down the service at 100,000 rows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for N+1 query patterns (querying in a loop). Are batch operations used? Are large result sets paginated? Are unnecessary columns selected?
baseContent.exampleComment: This loop executes one query per order ID - a classic N+1 problem. Use a single IN clause or batch query to fetch all orders at once.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// Single batch query
List<OrderDetail> details = repository.findAllById(orderIds); // 1 query

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Batch your queries. N+1 is the most common and most damaging query anti-pattern.

items.text: Are appropriate indexes in place for new queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New database queries should have supporting indexes. Without indexes, queries perform full table scans that get slower as data grows.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A missing index turns a millisecond query into a multi-second query at scale. Adding indexes after launch is an emergency fix; adding them during development is planning.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each new query, check that the WHERE clause columns and JOIN columns have indexes. Are composite indexes needed for multi-column filters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new query filters by customer_id and status but there is no composite index on (customer_id, status). This will be a full table scan at scale.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every new query needs an index review. Missing indexes are production incidents waiting to happen.

items.text: Is pagination used for large result sets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations that return potentially large result sets must be paginated. Loading all results into memory at once risks OutOfMemoryError and high latency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A query returning 10M rows into a List will exhaust heap memory. Pagination keeps memory usage bounded and response times predictable regardless of data size.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for queries or API calls that load all results. Are large lists paginated? Is there a reasonable default and maximum page size?
baseContent.exampleComment: This method loads all orders for a customer into memory. For power users with thousands of orders, this will cause high memory usage. Add pagination.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always paginate large result sets. What works for 100 rows breaks at 100,000.

items.text: Are caches used where appropriate, with proper invalidation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Caching should be used for frequently read, rarely changed data. Every cache must have a clear invalidation strategy (TTL, event-driven, or manual) to prevent serving stale data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A cache without invalidation serves stale data. A cache without TTL grows unbounded. A cache on the wrong data (frequently changing) wastes memory and adds complexity without benefit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new caches. What is the invalidation strategy? Is there a TTL? Is the cached data appropriate for caching (read-heavy, stable)? Are cache misses handled correctly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This cache has no TTL or invalidation mechanism. If the underlying data changes, stale data will be served indefinitely. Add a TTL or event-driven invalidation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every cache needs a TTL and size limit. Unbounded caches become memory leaks.

items.text: Are large proto messages avoided in hot paths (serialization cost)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto messages with many fields or large repeated fields should not be serialized/deserialized frequently in performance-critical code paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto serialization is not free. Large messages with deeply nested structures cause significant allocation and GC pressure. In hot paths, this adds latency and reduces throughput.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check proto messages used in high-throughput code. Are they minimal? Could a lighter message be used? Are repeated fields potentially unbounded?
baseContent.exampleComment: This hot path deserializes the full Order proto (with all items and metadata) just to read the status. Consider using a lighter OrderSummary proto or caching the status separately.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep proto messages small in hot paths. Deserialize the minimum needed.

items.text: Are `repeated` proto fields bounded in practice (max list sizes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Repeated (list) fields in proto messages should have practical bounds. A message with a repeated field that can contain millions of items will cause memory and performance issues.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto deserialization allocates memory for all repeated elements. An unbounded repeated field can cause OutOfMemoryError, high GC pressure, and extreme serialization times.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check repeated fields in new or modified protos. What is the expected maximum size? Is there server-side validation to enforce a limit?
baseContent.exampleComment: The repeated 'events' field has no practical bound. If a client sends 1M events in one message, the server will OOM. Add validation to limit list size.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate repeated field sizes. Unbounded lists are memory bombs waiting to explode.

items.text: Is lazy initialization used for expensive objects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Expensive objects (connections, caches, compiled patterns) should be created lazily - only when first needed - rather than eagerly at startup if they might not be used.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Eager initialization of expensive objects slows down startup, wastes memory for unused features, and can cause startup failures if a dependency is temporarily unavailable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for expensive object creation in constructors or field initializers. Are they always needed? Could they be lazily initialized on first use?
baseContent.exampleComment: This regex Pattern is compiled in the constructor but only used in one code path. Consider lazy initialization using a holder pattern or Suppliers.memoize().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Initialize expensive objects lazily when they might not be needed. Startup time and memory thank you.

items.text: Are connection pools, thread pools, and resource pools properly configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Connection pools, thread pools, and resource pools should be properly configured with appropriate sizes, timeouts, and monitoring.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misconfigured pools are a top cause of production incidents. Too small causes starvation and timeouts. Too large wastes resources and can overwhelm backends. No monitoring means you find out too late.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pool configurations. Are sizes appropriate for the workload? Are connection timeouts set? Is pool utilization monitored?
baseContent.exampleComment: This connection pool has no maximum size or connection timeout. Under load, it will create unlimited connections and overwhelm the database. Add maxPoolSize and connectionTimeout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure pools explicitly: size, timeouts, naming, and monitoring. Defaults are rarely right for production.

items.text: Are logging levels appropriate (`DEBUG` for verbose, `INFO` for operational, `WARN`/`ERROR` for problems)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log levels should match the severity: ERROR for failures needing attention, WARN for concerning but handled situations, INFO for important state changes, DEBUG for development details.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong log levels cause alert fatigue (too many ERRORs) or missing signals (important events at DEBUG). Consistent levels make log analysis and alerting reliable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new log statements. Is ERROR used only for things that need human attention? Is INFO used for significant events? Is DEBUG used for diagnostic details?
baseContent.exampleComment: This logs every successful request at INFO level. At high traffic, this will flood the logs. Consider DEBUG for routine success and INFO only for significant events.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
log.error("User not found: " + id); // not an error, expected case
log.info("Processing item " + item); // too noisy at INFO
log.debug("Service is down!"); // too quiet for a real problem

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
log.debug("User not found: {}", id); // expected, diagnostic
log.debug("Processing item {}", item); // routine, keep at DEBUG
log.error("Service {} is down, failing over", serviceName); // needs attention

baseContent.keyTakeaway: ERROR means wake someone up. WARN means investigate soon. INFO means noteworthy. DEBUG means diagnosis.

items.text: Is logging not done inside tight loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Avoid logging inside tight loops or high-frequency code paths. Each log call has overhead (string formatting, I/O, potential synchronization).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logging in a loop processing 100K items generates 100K log lines, consuming disk I/O, CPU for formatting, and disk space. It can slow the loop by orders of magnitude.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for log statements inside loops, stream operations, or frequently called methods. Could they be moved outside the loop or sampled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This INFO log inside the processing loop will generate millions of lines per day. Move it outside the loop or sample it (log every 1000th item).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log summaries, not individual items. Move logs outside loops or sample sparingly.

items.text: Is user input validated and sanitized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All user input must be validated for type, length, format, and range before processing. Input should be sanitized to prevent injection attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input is the root cause of most security vulnerabilities: SQL injection, XSS, command injection, and path traversal. Validation at the boundary is the first line of defense.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all points where user input enters the system. Is it validated? Are string lengths bounded? Are formats verified? Is special character handling correct?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'filename' parameter from the request is used directly in a file path without validation. This is vulnerable to path traversal (../../../etc/passwd).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all input at the boundary. Never trust user-provided data.

items.text: Are SQL queries parameterized (no string concatenation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SQL queries must use parameterized queries (prepared statements) instead of string concatenation to prevent SQL injection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection is one of the most critical and common vulnerabilities. String concatenation allows attackers to execute arbitrary SQL, potentially reading, modifying, or deleting all data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation in SQL queries. Are all user-provided values passed as parameters? Are there any raw SQL strings with concatenated variables?
baseContent.exampleComment: This query concatenates the user ID directly into the SQL string. This is a SQL injection vulnerability. Use a parameterized query with a PreparedStatement.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use parameterized queries. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

items.text: Are sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, PII) not logged?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sensitive data (passwords, tokens, API keys, PII like SSN or email) must never appear in logs, error messages, or debug output.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logs are often stored in less-secure systems, accessed by many people, and retained for long periods. Sensitive data in logs creates compliance violations and security breaches.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check log statements and error messages. Do they include request bodies, headers, or user data that might contain sensitive fields? Are proto toString outputs filtered?
baseContent.exampleComment: This log statement includes the full request proto, which contains the user's auth token. Either exclude sensitive fields or use a masked toString method.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
log.info("Login request: {}", request); // logs password!
log.error("Auth failed for token: {}", authToken); // logs token!
log.debug("User data: {}", user.toString()); // logs PII!

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never log sensitive data. Log identifiers and metadata, not secrets and PII.

items.text: Are authentication and authorization checks in place for new endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every endpoint must verify who is making the request (authentication) and whether they are allowed to perform the action (authorization).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing auth checks mean anyone can access any endpoint. Missing authz checks mean authenticated users can access resources they should not. Both are critical vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new endpoints and RPC handlers. Are they protected by authentication middleware? Are authorization checks (role-based, resource-based) applied before processing?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new endpoint has no authorization check. An authenticated user could modify any customer's orders, not just their own. Add ownership verification.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every endpoint needs authentication and authorization. Check both identity and permissions.

items.text: Are secrets not hardcoded (use environment variables or secret managers)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens, certificates) must never be hardcoded in source code. They should come from environment variables, secret managers, or encrypted config.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets in source code are visible to everyone with repository access, persist in git history forever, and cannot be rotated without a code change and deployment.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings that look like API keys, passwords, or tokens. Check for hardcoded URLs with credentials. Verify secrets come from configuration or a secret manager.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API key is hardcoded in the source file. It should be loaded from a secret manager or environment variable so it can be rotated without code changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
private static final String API_KEY = "<hardcoded-secret>";
private static final String DB_PASSWORD = "p@ssw0rd";

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never hardcode secrets. Use environment variables or secret managers.

items.text: Are dependency versions free of known vulnerabilities?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Third-party dependencies should be checked for known security vulnerabilities (CVEs). Use tools like Dependabot, Snyk, or OWASP Dependency-Check.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Vulnerable dependencies are one of the most exploited attack vectors. A single outdated library can expose your entire service to remote code execution, data leaks, or denial of service.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new or changed dependencies. Are they on recent versions? Do they have known CVEs? Is automated vulnerability scanning configured in CI?
baseContent.exampleComment: The log4j version 2.14.0 has a critical RCE vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228). Please upgrade to 2.17.1 or later.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep dependencies updated. Automate vulnerability scanning in CI.

items.text: Are proto fields containing sensitive data annotated or documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto fields that contain sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data) should be annotated or documented so that logging, tracing, and serialization can handle them appropriately.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without annotation, sensitive proto fields are easily logged or transmitted to analytics systems. Annotations enable automated redaction in logging frameworks and compliance audits.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new proto fields. Do any contain PII, credentials, or financial data? Are they documented as sensitive? Is there a mechanism to redact them in logs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'social_security_number' field should be annotated as sensitive. Our logging framework redacts fields with this annotation to prevent PII leakage.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Annotate sensitive proto fields. It enables automated redaction and compliance.

items.text: Is data encrypted in transit (TLS) and at rest where required?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data should be encrypted in transit (TLS for gRPC and HTTP) and at rest (encrypted storage for sensitive data). Verify TLS configuration is correct and up to date.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unencrypted data in transit can be intercepted by network attackers. Unencrypted data at rest is exposed if storage is compromised. Encryption is a fundamental security control.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check gRPC channel configuration. Is TLS enabled? Are certificates configured correctly? For stored data, is encryption enabled? Are encryption keys managed properly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This gRPC channel is created with plaintext transport. For production, use TLS: ManagedChannelBuilder.forAddress(...).useTransportSecurity().build().

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
ManagedChannel channel = ManagedChannelBuilder
    .forAddress("payment-service", 8080)
    .usePlaintext() // NO encryption!
    .build();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
ManagedChannel channel = ManagedChannelBuilder
    .forAddress("payment-service", 8443)
    .useTransportSecurity()
    .build();

// Or with custom TLS config:
ManagedChannel channel = NettyChannelBuilder
    .forAddress("payment-service", 8443)
    .sslContext(GrpcSslContexts.forClient()
        .trustManager(certFile)
        .build())
    .build();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use TLS for gRPC and HTTP in production. Encrypt sensitive data at rest.

items.text: Are new metrics, traces, or logs added for new features?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features and code paths should include observability instrumentation: metrics for monitoring, traces for debugging latency, and structured logs for troubleshooting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code without observability is a black box in production. When something goes wrong, you need metrics to detect it, traces to isolate it, and logs to diagnose it.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new code paths. Are there metrics for success/failure counts and latencies? Are trace spans added for significant operations? Are important state transitions logged?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new payment processing path has no metrics. Adding a counter for success/failure and a timer for latency would help us monitor it in production.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Instrument new code with metrics, traces, and structured logs. Observability is not optional.

items.text: Are structured logging fields used (not string interpolation in log messages)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log messages should use structured fields (key-value pairs) instead of string interpolation, enabling efficient searching and filtering in log aggregation systems.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unstructured logs like 'Processing order 123 for customer 456' require regex to search. Structured logs with orderId=123, customerId=456 enable instant filtering in tools like Splunk or Elasticsearch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check log statements. Are they using SLF4J parameterized logging? Are structured fields added via MDC or key-value pairs? Can important fields be filtered in the log aggregation tool?
baseContent.exampleComment: This log uses string concatenation. Use SLF4J's parameterized logging with structured fields: log.info("Order processed", kv("orderId", id), kv("duration", ms)).

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
log.info("Processing order " + orderId + " for customer " + customerId
    + " total=" + total); // string concatenation, unstructured

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use structured logging. Searchable key-value pairs beat grepping through unstructured text.

items.text: Are alerts or dashboards updated for new failure modes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When adding new features, metrics, or changing critical paths, ensure monitoring dashboards and alerting rules are updated to cover the new functionality.
baseContent.whyItMatters: New code paths without alerts are invisible to operations. If the new payment method fails, but alerts only cover the old payment method, nobody gets paged.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there new metrics that need dashboard panels? Do existing alerts cover the new code paths? Are SLOs updated if the change affects latency or availability?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new retry mechanism emits metrics but no alert is configured. If retries spike, we should be alerted. Could you add an alert for retry rate > 10%?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: New metrics need alerts. New features need dashboard panels. Monitor what you ship.

items.text: Are feature flags used for gradual rollout of risky changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Significant changes should be gated behind feature flags so they can be gradually rolled out and quickly disabled without a deployment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Feature flags decouple deployment from release. If a new feature causes problems, you can disable it instantly without rolling back the deployment.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the new feature behind a feature flag? Can it be disabled without a deployment? Is the flag configuration documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new processing algorithm is a significant change. Could we gate it behind a feature flag so we can roll it out gradually and quickly revert if needed?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use feature flags for significant changes. They provide instant rollback without deployment.

items.text: Are migration scripts idempotent and backward compatible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database migration scripts should be idempotent (safe to run multiple times) and backward compatible (old code still works during migration).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Migrations run during deployments when both old and new code is running. Non-idempotent migrations fail on retry. Non-backward-compatible migrations break old code during rollout.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check migration scripts. Would running them twice cause errors? Can old code still function after the migration? Are there IF NOT EXISTS guards?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a NOT NULL column without a default value. Old code cannot insert rows during the migration window. Add a default value or make it nullable first.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Migrations must be idempotent and backward compatible. Split breaking changes into multiple steps.

items.text: Is there a rollback plan for the change?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every significant change should have a documented plan for rolling back if problems are discovered after deployment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a rollback plan, problems in production become emergencies. With a plan, they are controlled responses. Thinking about rollback also reveals hidden risks in the change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Can this change be reverted by simply rolling back the deployment? Are there database migrations that need reversal? Are there one-way data changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration is irreversible - it drops a column. What is the rollback plan if something goes wrong? Could we rename the column instead of dropping it?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Plan for failure. Every deployment should have a documented rollback strategy.

items.text: Are health checks updated if dependencies change?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the service adds or changes dependencies (new databases, services, message queues), health checks should be updated to verify these dependencies are available.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Health checks that do not cover all dependencies give false confidence. The service reports healthy while a critical dependency is down, causing requests to fail.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the change add new external dependencies? Are health checks updated to include them? Do readiness probes verify that all dependencies are reachable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new Redis cache dependency but the health check does not include it. If Redis goes down, requests will fail but the service will still report healthy.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Health checks must cover all dependencies. Update them when dependencies change.

items.text: Are Javadoc comments added for public APIs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All public classes, methods, and interfaces should have Javadoc comments explaining their purpose, parameters, return values, and thrown exceptions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Javadoc is the primary way Java developers discover and understand APIs. Without it, consumers must read the source code, which defeats the purpose of abstraction.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new public classes and methods. Do they have Javadoc? Are @param, @return, and @throws tags present? Is the description useful (not just restating the name)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This public interface has no Javadoc. Consumers will not know the contract, thread-safety guarantees, or expected behavior. Please add Javadoc for the interface and its methods.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Javadoc public APIs. Describe the contract, not the implementation.

items.text: Are proto field comments clear about semantics, units, and constraints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto field comments should explain what the field means, its valid values, constraints, and how it relates to other fields. Comments are the API documentation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto schemas are shared across teams and languages. Without clear comments, each team interprets fields differently, leading to subtle integration bugs and data inconsistencies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check proto field comments. Do they explain the semantics? Are constraints documented (max length, valid values, required vs optional)? Are relationships between fields clear?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'status' field has no comment. Consumers need to know the valid values, transitions, and whether it is set by the client or server.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Proto field comments are API documentation. Be specific about semantics, constraints, and defaults.

items.text: Are complex algorithms or business rules explained in comments?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Complex algorithms, non-obvious business rules, and tricky code should have explanatory comments. The code says what; comments say why.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Six months from now, nobody (including you) will remember why the discount calculation has a special case for Tuesdays. Comments on complex logic prevent misconceptions and incorrect modifications.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for complex logic: nested conditionals, business rules, mathematical formulas, or performance optimizations. Are they explained with comments?
baseContent.exampleComment: This pricing logic has three special cases but no comments explaining why. Could you add comments explaining the business rules behind each case?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Comment the why, not the what. Business rules and non-obvious logic need explanation.

items.text: Is the README or internal wiki updated for architectural changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the change affects how the service is configured, deployed, or used, the README, runbook, or internal wiki should be updated to reflect the change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Outdated documentation sends developers down wrong paths, causes configuration errors, and wastes time. Documentation is only useful if it is kept current.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the change introduce new configuration, new dependencies, or changed behavior? Is the README or wiki updated? Are deployment instructions still accurate?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new required environment variable (REDIS_URL) but the README does not mention it. New developers will get a cryptic startup error. Please update the setup instructions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update docs when you change behavior. Outdated documentation is worse than no documentation.

items.text: Are breaking changes called out in the PR description?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Breaking changes (API changes, schema changes, configuration changes) must be clearly called out in the PR description with migration instructions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking changes that slip in unannounced cause outages for downstream teams. Calling them out enables coordinated migration and prevents surprises.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the PR modify public APIs, proto schemas, or configuration? Is the breaking change documented in the PR description? Are migration instructions provided?
baseContent.exampleComment: This removes a proto field that other teams depend on. Could you add a BREAKING CHANGE section to the PR description with migration instructions?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Call out breaking changes loudly. Provide migration instructions. No surprises for consumers.
